name: Vietnam
tagline: A thousand years under Chinese rule, a thousand more of fiercely defended independence, and a modern nation forged in war and reform.
summary: Vietnamese history stretches from the legendary kingdoms of the Red River delta to one of Asia's most dynamic modern economies. Tradition traces the nation to Van Lang and the Hung kings and to the kingdom of Au Lac, but the verifiable story begins with more than a millennium of Chinese domination, from the Han conquest in 111 BCE until Ngo Quyen's victory on the Bach Dang River in 938 CE. The independent dynasties that followed, the Ly, Tran, and Le, built a Confucian state, expanded steadily southward at the expense of Champa, and repeatedly threw back invaders, including three Mongol assaults under Kublai Khan. The Nguyen dynasty unified the long country under Gia Long in 1802, only to fall under French colonial rule within the French Indochina established in 1887. On 2 September 1945 Ho Chi Minh proclaimed the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, opening three decades of war: against France, then in a divided nation through the Vietnam War, until reunification in 1975 and 1976. Since the Doi Moi reforms of 1986, Vietnam has transformed itself into a fast-growing, globally integrated state while remaining a one-party socialist republic.

founding.label: Declaration of Independence of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam
founding.detail: On 2 September 1945, in Hanoi's Ba Dinh Square, Ho Chi Minh read the Declaration of Independence proclaiming the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, echoing the American and French declarations of rights. The act came after the long arc of Vietnamese statehood: the legendary kingdoms of Van Lang and Au Lac ruled by the Hung kings and An Duong Vuong, roughly a thousand years of Chinese rule from 111 BCE until Ngo Quyen's victory at Bach Dang in 938 CE, and centuries of independent dynasties. Independence in 1945 was not the end of the struggle; war with France and then the division of the country gave way only after 1975 to the reunified Socialist Republic of Vietnam, formally proclaimed on 2 July 1976.

quickFacts.label: Legendary founding
quickFacts.value: Van Lang under the Hung kings, traditionally c. 2879 BCE (mythical)

quickFacts.label: Chinese rule
quickFacts.value: From the Han conquest in 111 BCE to Ngo Quyen's victory in 938 CE, roughly a millennium

quickFacts.label: End of monarchy
quickFacts.value: The Nguyen dynasty, last imperial house, abdicated in 1945

quickFacts.label: Division of the country
quickFacts.value: Split at the 17th parallel by the 1954 Geneva Accords until reunification

quickFacts.label: Reunification
quickFacts.value: Socialist Republic of Vietnam proclaimed 2 July 1976

quickFacts.label: Economic turning point
quickFacts.value: Doi Moi market reforms launched in 1986

eras.title: Legendary Origins and a Thousand Years of Chinese Rule
eras.standfirst: From the rice paddies of the Red River delta emerged the legendary kingdoms of the Hung kings, but the verifiable nation was forged in more than a thousand years of resistance to Chinese imperial rule.
body: Vietnamese tradition traces the nation to Van Lang, the Land of the Tattooed Men, ruled by a line of eighteen Hung kings descended from the dragon lord Lac Long Quan and the fairy Au Co. The legendary chronology assigns its founding to 2879 BCE, but archaeology supports only a Bronze Age polity in the Red River basin in the first millennium BCE, associated with the sophisticated Dong Son culture and its great bronze drums. According to tradition the last Hung king was overthrown in the 3rd century BCE by An Duong Vuong, who united the realm as Au Lac and built the spiral citadel of Co Loa north of present-day Hanoi.

In 111 BCE the armies of Han China absorbed the region, beginning a period of direct Chinese rule that, with brief interruptions, lasted more than a thousand years. Chinese administration brought Confucian government, the writing system, Mahayana Buddhism, and intensive wet-rice agriculture, but also heavy taxation and forced assimilation that bred recurring revolt. In 40 CE the Trung sisters, Trung Trac and Trung Nhi, led a rebellion that briefly expelled the Chinese before being crushed in 43 CE; they remain enduring national heroines, as does Lady Trieu, who rose against the rulers in the 3rd century.

Centuries of intermittent uprising finally bore fruit amid the collapse of the Tang dynasty in China. In 938 the general Ngo Quyen destroyed a Southern Han invasion fleet at the mouth of the Bach Dang River, famously impaling the enemy ships on iron-tipped stakes revealed by the falling tide. The victory ended the long era of Chinese domination, and in 939 Ngo Quyen proclaimed himself king, inaugurating the independent Vietnamese state.

events.title: Legendary founding of Van Lang
events.summary: Vietnamese tradition dates the kingdom of Van Lang and its line of Hung kings to 2879 BCE; the historical Bronze Age Dong Son culture of the Red River delta flourished in the first millennium BCE.

events.title: Kingdom of Au Lac and Co Loa
events.summary: An Duong Vuong overthrows the last Hung king and unites the land as Au Lac, building the fortified capital of Co Loa north of modern Hanoi.

events.title: Han conquest begins Chinese rule
events.summary: The Chinese Han dynasty conquers the region, opening more than a thousand years of direct Chinese administration, taxation, and cultural influence.

events.title: Revolt of the Trung sisters
events.summary: Trung Trac and Trung Nhi lead a rebellion that briefly drives out the Chinese before being defeated in 43 CE; they become lasting symbols of Vietnamese resistance.

events.title: Battle of Bach Dang and independence
events.summary: Ngo Quyen destroys a Southern Han fleet at the Bach Dang River using hidden iron-tipped stakes, ending Chinese rule; he proclaims himself king in 939.

figures.name: Trung Sisters (Hai Ba Trung)
figures.blurb: Sisters Trung Trac and Trung Nhi raised a revolt in 40 CE that briefly liberated dozens of districts from Chinese rule and established a short-lived independent state; defeated in 43 CE, they became the most celebrated heroines of Vietnamese national memory.

figures.name: Ngo Quyen
figures.blurb: His decisive victory over the Southern Han at the Bach Dang River in 938 ended roughly a millennium of Chinese domination; proclaiming himself king the following year, he is regarded as the founder of Vietnamese independence.

pullquote.text: The Trung sisters and Ngo Quyen stand at the headwaters of a national tradition defined by resistance to foreign domination.

eras.title: Independent Dynasties: Ly, Tran, and Le
eras.standfirst: Under the Ly, Tran, and Le dynasties, Vietnam built an enduring Confucian state, repelled the Mongols three times, and expelled Ming China, while pushing its frontier steadily southward.
body: The lasting framework of independent Vietnam was laid by the Ly dynasty, founded in 1009 when the official Ly Cong Uan took the throne. The Ly moved the capital to Thang Long, modern Hanoi, in 1010, established Confucian examinations and a Buddhist court culture, codified law, and named the realm Dai Viet. They also began the long process known as the Nam tien, the march south, expanding at the expense of the Indianized kingdom of Champa.

The Tran dynasty, which succeeded the Ly in 1225, faced the gravest external threat in Vietnamese history when the Mongol empire of Kublai Khan invaded three times. Under the brilliant general Tran Hung Dao, the Vietnamese repelled the Mongols in 1258, again in 1285, and decisively in 1288, when Tran Hung Dao revived Ngo Quyen's stake tactic to annihilate the Mongol fleet at a second Battle of Bach Dang. These victories secured Vietnam's independence and entered the national epic.

After a period of weakness, Ming China occupied Dai Viet in 1407, but the landowner Le Loi raised a ten-year resistance and expelled the Chinese, founding the Le dynasty in 1428. The Le presided over a golden age of Confucian governance, marked by the comprehensive Hong Duc legal code of Emperor Le Thanh Tong and the final conquest of Champa's capital in 1471. From the 16th century, however, the Le emperors became figureheads as the rival Trinh lords in the north and Nguyen lords in the south divided real power, a partition that endured until the Tay Son uprising overthrew the old order in the 1770s and 1780s.

events.title: Founding of the Ly dynasty
events.summary: Ly Cong Uan takes the throne and founds the Ly dynasty, moving the capital to Thang Long (Hanoi) in 1010 and establishing the enduring institutions of independent Dai Viet.

events.title: Tran defeat of the Mongol invasions
events.summary: Under Tran Hung Dao the Tran dynasty repels three invasions by Kublai Khan's Mongols, climaxing in the 1288 victory at the Bach Dang River where the Mongol fleet is destroyed on hidden stakes.

events.title: Ming China occupies Dai Viet
events.summary: The Chinese Ming dynasty conquers Dai Viet and imposes two decades of harsh direct rule, provoking widespread resistance.

events.title: Le Loi expels the Ming and founds the Le dynasty
events.summary: After a ten-year war of liberation, Le Loi drives out the Ming, restores independence, and establishes the Le dynasty, which would rule, nominally, for centuries.

events.title: Conquest of Champa's capital
events.summary: Emperor Le Thanh Tong's forces capture Vijaya, the capital of Champa, accelerating the southward expansion (Nam tien) of Vietnamese settlement.

figures.name: Tran Hung Dao
figures.blurb: The supreme commander who defeated the Mongol invasions of the 13th century, most famously by destroying Kublai Khan's fleet at the Bach Dang River in 1288; revered ever since as one of Vietnam's greatest military strategists.

figures.name: Le Loi (Le Thai To)
figures.blurb: A landowner from Thanh Hoa who led a decade-long insurgency against Ming occupation and, victorious in 1428, founded the Le dynasty and restored the independence of Dai Viet.

pullquote.text: Three times the Mongols came, and three times Dai Viet, under Tran Hung Dao, turned them back.

eras.title: The Nguyen Dynasty and Expansion South
eras.standfirst: Emerging from the chaos of the Tay Son rebellion, Nguyen Anh reunified a country stretched from the Red River to the Mekong delta, founding in 1802 the last imperial dynasty of Vietnam.
body: The Tay Son uprising, which began in 1771 and toppled both the Nguyen and Trinh lords as well as the figurehead Le emperors, threw Vietnam into upheaval. Its leader Nguyen Hue crushed a Chinese Qing intervention near Thang Long in 1789, but Tay Son rule proved short-lived. Nguyen Anh, a surviving prince of the southern Nguyen line, rallied support in the Mekong delta, secured French military assistance, and over years of warfare defeated the Tay Son.

In 1802 Nguyen Anh proclaimed himself emperor under the reign name Gia Long, taken from Gia Dinh (Saigon) in the south and Thang Long (Hanoi) in the north to symbolize a country at last unified from end to end. He established his capital at Hue and, in 1804, the Chinese court recognized the realm under the name Vietnam. The new dynasty crowned the centuries-long Nam tien, the southward expansion that had carried Vietnamese settlement across former Cham and Khmer lands all the way to the Gulf of Thailand.

Gia Long and his successors, notably Minh Mang, built a centralized Confucian bureaucracy modeled closely on Qing China, compiled comprehensive law codes, and promoted classical learning. They also grew increasingly wary of European influence and Catholic missionary activity, periodically persecuting Christian converts. This hostility, together with French commercial and strategic ambitions, set the stage for foreign intervention: in 1858 a French naval expedition attacked Da Nang, opening the conquest that would end Vietnamese sovereignty.

events.title: Tay Son rebellion
events.summary: A peasant-led uprising overthrows the Nguyen and Trinh lords and the Le dynasty, and in 1789 routs an invading Chinese Qing army near Thang Long.

events.title: Gia Long founds the Nguyen dynasty
events.summary: Nguyen Anh defeats the Tay Son, reunifies the country, and is crowned emperor as Gia Long at Hue, founding Vietnam's last imperial dynasty.

events.title: The name Vietnam adopted
events.summary: The unified realm is recognized under the name Vietnam, with its capital at Hue and a centralized administration modeled on Qing China.

events.title: Reign of Minh Mang
events.summary: Emperor Minh Mang consolidates the Confucian state, completes administrative reforms, and intensifies suspicion of European traders and Catholic missionaries.

events.title: French attack on Da Nang
events.summary: A French and Spanish naval force assaults Da Nang, beginning the military conquest that would bring Vietnam under colonial rule.

figures.name: Gia Long (Nguyen Anh)
figures.role: Founding emperor of the Nguyen dynasty
figures.blurb: After decades of war against the Tay Son, he reunified Vietnam in 1802 and ruled as its first Nguyen emperor from Hue, his reign name fusing the names of Saigon and Hanoi to proclaim a country whole from south to north.

pullquote.text: Gia, from Gia Dinh in the south, and Long, from Thang Long in the north: a reign name for a country at last made one.

eras.title: French Indochina
eras.standfirst: Conquered piece by piece, Vietnam was divided into the colony of Cochinchina and the protectorates of Annam and Tonkin, bound into French Indochina, where colonial exploitation bred a rising tide of nationalism.
body: French conquest advanced from south to north over three decades. France seized Saigon in 1859 and completed the annexation of southern Cochinchina by 1862; over the 1880s it imposed protectorates over central Annam and northern Tonkin. In 1887 France formally organized these Vietnamese territories together with Cambodia into the Indochinese Union, French Indochina, adding Laos in 1893. Although Cochinchina was ruled as a direct colony and Annam and Tonkin nominally as protectorates under the powerless Nguyen emperors, French authority was pervasive throughout.

Colonial rule reshaped the economy to serve French interests. The administration developed rubber plantations, coal mines, and rice exports from the Mekong delta, built railways and the city of Hanoi as the capital of Indochina, and imposed monopolies on salt, alcohol, and opium. The benefits flowed overwhelmingly to French settlers and a small Vietnamese elite, while peasants bore heavy taxes and harsh plantation labor.

Resistance was continuous, evolving from the scholar-led Can Vuong movement of the 1880s into modern nationalism in the 20th century. Reformers such as Phan Boi Chau looked abroad for models, while a young Nguyen Tat Thanh, later known as Ho Chi Minh, turned to communism and in 1930 helped found the Indochinese Communist Party. During the Second World War, Japan occupied Indochina while leaving the French administration nominally in place until March 1945. Amid the resulting power vacuum and a devastating famine that killed hundreds of thousands, Ho Chi Minh's Viet Minh seized the moment of Japan's surrender to launch the August Revolution.

events.title: France annexes Cochinchina
events.summary: Following the capture of Saigon, France secures the southern provinces of Cochinchina as a colony, its first permanent foothold in Vietnam.

events.title: French Indochina established
events.summary: France unites Cochinchina, Annam, Tonkin, and Cambodia into the Indochinese Union; Laos is added in 1893, binding Vietnam into a colonial federation centered on Hanoi.

events.title: Founding of the Indochinese Communist Party
events.summary: Ho Chi Minh helps unite rival communist groups into the Indochinese Communist Party, which becomes the organizational core of the independence struggle.

events.title: Great Vietnamese Famine
events.summary: Under Japanese occupation and wartime mismanagement, famine strikes northern Vietnam, killing an estimated one to two million people and deepening revolutionary discontent.

events.title: Declaration of independence
events.summary: As Japan surrenders, the Viet Minh seize power in the August Revolution and Ho Chi Minh proclaims the Democratic Republic of Vietnam in Hanoi's Ba Dinh Square.

figures.name: Ho Chi Minh
figures.blurb: Born Nguyen Sinh Cung, he spent decades abroad as a communist organizer before leading the Viet Minh, proclaiming Vietnamese independence in 1945, and serving as president of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam through the wars against France and the United States.

pullquote.text: All men are created equal; they are endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable rights.
pullquote.attribution: Ho Chi Minh, quoting the U.S. Declaration of Independence, 2 September 1945

eras.title: The Wars: Independence, Division, and Reunification
eras.standfirst: Three decades of war, against France, between a divided North and South, and against the United States, cost millions of Vietnamese lives before the country was reunified in 1975 and 1976.
body: France refused to accept Vietnamese independence, and the First Indochina War erupted in 1946. For eight years the Viet Minh, led militarily by General Vo Nguyen Giap, waged a war of attrition that culminated in the spectacular siege and capture of the French base at Dien Bien Phu, which fell on 7 May 1954. The Geneva Accords that July ended French rule but temporarily divided the country at the 17th parallel, with Ho Chi Minh's communist government in the North and a U.S.-backed anti-communist state under Ngo Dinh Diem in the South. The promised reunification elections were never held.

The division hardened into the Vietnam War. A communist-led insurgency in the South, supported by Hanoi, escalated through the early 1960s, and after the Gulf of Tonkin incident in 1964 the United States committed massive ground forces, eventually more than half a million troops, alongside a sustained bombing campaign. The war was catastrophic in human terms: estimates of Vietnamese deaths range widely, with commonly cited figures of roughly two to three million Vietnamese killed, the majority of them civilians, in addition to vast numbers of wounded and displaced and lasting damage from defoliants such as Agent Orange. More than 58,000 American service members also died.

The 1968 Tet Offensive, though a military setback for the communists, shattered American confidence in the war and accelerated U.S. disengagement. The Paris Peace Accords of January 1973 provided for the withdrawal of American forces, but fighting between North and South continued. In the spring of 1975 a North Vietnamese offensive swept south, and on 30 April 1975 Saigon fell, ending the war. The country was formally reunified as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam on 2 July 1976, with its capital at Hanoi.

events.title: First Indochina War
events.summary: War between France and the Viet Minh ends with the French defeat at Dien Bien Phu on 7 May 1954, terminating French colonial rule in Vietnam.

events.title: Geneva Accords divide Vietnam
events.summary: The Geneva Conference partitions Vietnam at the 17th parallel into a communist North and an anti-communist South pending reunification elections that are never held.

events.title: Gulf of Tonkin and U.S. escalation
events.summary: The Gulf of Tonkin incident leads Congress to pass a resolution authorizing the vast escalation of direct U.S. military involvement in the war.

events.title: Tet Offensive
events.summary: A coordinated communist surprise offensive across South Vietnam, though costly militarily, undermines American public support and shifts the war toward U.S. withdrawal.

events.title: Fall of Saigon and reunification
events.summary: North Vietnamese forces capture Saigon, ending the war; the country is formally reunified as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam on 2 July 1976.

figures.name: Vo Nguyen Giap
figures.blurb: The self-taught military strategist who built the People's Army of Vietnam, masterminded the victory at Dien Bien Phu in 1954, and led communist forces through the Vietnam War to the fall of Saigon in 1975.

figures.name: Ngo Dinh Diem
figures.blurb: The Catholic anti-communist nationalist who led the U.S.-backed Republic of Vietnam from 1955, his authoritarian and divisive rule ended in his assassination during a 1963 coup.

pullquote.text: Estimates of Vietnamese war dead reach into the millions, the great majority of them civilians.
pullquote.attribution: Terralore, on the human cost of the war

eras.title: Doi Moi Reforms and Modern Vietnam
eras.standfirst: After years of postwar hardship and isolation, the Doi Moi reforms of 1986 opened Vietnam's economy and launched its transformation into one of the world's fastest-growing nations.
body: The first postwar decade was grim. Collectivization and central planning, compounded by a U.S. trade embargo, a border war with China in 1979, costly occupation of Cambodia, and the exodus of hundreds of thousands of refugees, left Vietnam impoverished and internationally isolated. By the mid-1980s inflation had soared past 700 percent and the economy stood on the brink of collapse.

At its Sixth Party Congress in December 1986, the Communist Party of Vietnam launched Doi Moi, or renovation, abandoning rigid central planning for a socialist-oriented market economy. The reforms decollectivized agriculture and restored family farming, legalized private enterprise, welcomed foreign investment, and reopened the country to global trade. The results were dramatic: Vietnam swiftly became one of the world's leading rice and coffee exporters, tamed hyperinflation, and sustained rapid growth that lifted tens of millions out of poverty.

Vietnam steadily rejoined the international community: it withdrew from Cambodia, normalized relations with China and, in 1995, with the United States, joined ASEAN that same year, and later the World Trade Organization in 2007. Today Vietnam is a one-party socialist republic with a fast-growing, export-driven economy, a young population, and an increasingly prominent role in regional affairs, even as it navigates tensions in the South China Sea and the pressures of rapid development.

events.title: Sino-Vietnamese War
events.summary: China invades northern Vietnam in response to Vietnam's ousting of the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia; the brief but bloody border war deepens Vietnam's isolation.

events.title: Doi Moi reforms launched
events.summary: The Communist Party's Sixth Congress adopts Doi Moi, replacing central planning with a socialist-oriented market economy and opening Vietnam to private enterprise and foreign investment.

events.title: Normalization with the U.S. and ASEAN membership
events.summary: Vietnam restores diplomatic relations with the United States and joins the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, ending decades of isolation.

events.title: Accession to the World Trade Organization
events.summary: Vietnam joins the WTO, cementing its integration into the global economy and accelerating an export-driven manufacturing boom.

figures.name: Nguyen Van Linh
figures.blurb: As general secretary of the Communist Party of Vietnam from 1986, he became the leading political champion of the Doi Moi reforms that opened the country's economy.

pullquote.text: Doi Moi, renovation, turned a country crippled by hyperinflation into one of the world's fastest-growing economies.

figures.name: Ho Chi Minh
figures.role: Founding president of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam
figures.blurb: The dominant figure of modern Vietnamese history, he led the independence movement, proclaimed the republic in 1945, and presided over the wars against France and the United States; the former Saigon was renamed Ho Chi Minh City in his honor.

figures.name: Tran Hung Dao
figures.blurb: Commander who repelled three Mongol invasions in the 13th century, his victory at Bach Dang in 1288 became a defining symbol of Vietnamese military resistance.

sources.label: Proclamation of Independence of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam (1945)
Dien Bien Phu and the Fall of French Indochina, 1954
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
